skill.name: skywork-ppt
skill.description: Generate, imitate, edit, and locally manipulate PowerPoint presentations with Skywork backend services and Python PPTX utilities.
skill.summary: Create new PPTX decks, adapt templates, edit existing presentations, and perform local slide operations.
skill.author: SkyworkAI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis findings for network, filesystem, environment access, and external commands are mostly true positives for the skill design. I found no evidence of prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent, but the skill handles persistent tokens and uploads user files to external Skywork services, so publication needs human review.

high_findings.title: Plaintext Token Cache In Home Directory
high_findings.description: The authentication helper stores Skywork token data in ~/.skywork_token and reloads it later. This creates persistent credential exposure if the host or home directory is shared.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly expands a hidden home path, writes token data as JSON, and later trusts the cached token. This is a real credential persistence risk, even if intended for login convenience.

high_findings.title: User Files Uploaded To External Services
high_findings.description: The skill uploads local documents, templates, and PPTX files to Skywork endpoints for parsing, storage, generation, and editing. This can expose confidential presentation content.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network upload and generation calls are central to the scripts and include authentication tokens. The behavior is documented but still high risk for sensitive user files.

medium_findings.title: External Browser Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The login flow launches a browser using platform commands and uses shell execution on Windows. The URL is assembled from service and environment-controlled values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are intended to open a login page, not run arbitrary user input. Risk remains because one branch uses shell=True and the login base can be environment overridden.

medium_findings.title: Local PPTX Operations Can Overwrite Files
medium_findings.description: Local slide operations can save over the original PPTX when no output path is provided. This is expected behavior but creates data loss risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The overwrite path is explicit and tied to user-requested local edits. It is not malicious, but marketplace users need warning before destructive operations.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Search Queries Become Temp File Names
medium_findings.description: The web search helper writes result files using the raw query text in the filename. This can fail or create unexpected paths for unusual query strings.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The directory is created with tempfile.mkdtemp, which limits impact. The filename still derives from user input without normalization.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The repeated weak cryptography alerts appear to match PPTX, XML, slide, or example text patterns. I found no cryptographic algorithm implementation in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe slides, PPTX commands, or examples, not hash or cipher usage. No evidence found for MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak primitives.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Alert Is Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The path traversal alert points to an example return structure containing a local path and a CDN URL. It is not executable path joining logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced code is a docstring explaining output format. Actual upload filenames use os.path.basename before multipart construction.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Match Expected Service Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Skywork API, web login, and example CDN endpoints needed for this PowerPoint service integration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domains and examples align with the skill purpose. They are still relevant disclosure points because requests leave the local machine.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Plus Network Combination
dangerous_patterns.description: Authentication tokens are read from the environment or cache and then attached to outbound API requests. This confirms the credential and network risk combination.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code obtains tokens and sends them in request headers. This is required functionality, but it confirms a sensitive data path.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Read And Remote Upload Path
dangerous_patterns.description: Local files are opened from user-supplied paths and sent to remote parsing or storage endpoints. Sensitive local files could be uploaded if selected accidentally.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The upload behavior is explicit and semantically confirmed by the script names and workflow. The user must provide paths, which lowers maliciousness but not exposure risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based Login Helper
dangerous_patterns.description: The Windows login branch invokes a shell command to open a URL. Shell execution should be avoided or tightly constrained in marketplace skills.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command target is a browser URL, not arbitrary command text. Confidence is lower because exploitability depends on Windows shell behavior and environment-controlled URL values.

content.value_statement: Building polished presentations from scratch or existing files can take many manual steps. This skill connects Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to Skywork services and local PPTX tools.
actual_capabilities: Generates new PPTX presentations from a topic, requirements, reference files, or web search summaries.
Creates a new presentation using an existing PPTX as a layout or style template.
Edits an existing PPTX from a public or uploaded URL using natural language instructions.
Uploads local reference files for parsing and extraction before presentation generation.
Performs local slide operations including info, delete, reorder, extract, and merge.
limitations: Skywork authentication is required for generation, template imitation, file parsing, upload, and edit workflows.
Generation and edit workflows send content to external Skywork services.
Large jobs may take about ten minutes and require progress log polling.
Local merge operations may lose complex animations, videos, or theme details.

use_cases.title: Executive Briefing Decks
use_cases.description: Turn a topic, source notes, or market research into a structured PPTX for leadership review.

use_cases.title: Template-Based Client Presentations
use_cases.description: Reuse a branded PPTX template while generating new slides for a different client or topic.

use_cases.title: Fast Slide Cleanup
use_cases.description: Delete, reorder, extract, or merge slides locally without sending the deck to a backend.

prompt_templates.title: Generate A Simple Deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an 8-slide English PPTX about renewable energy basics for a general audience. Use a clean business style.

prompt_templates.title: Use Source Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 10-slide presentation from the attached report. Preserve key numbers, dates, and recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Imitate A Template
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this PPTX template and create a new 12-slide deck about our Q2 product roadmap.

prompt_templates.title: Edit And Restructure A Deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this PPTX: add one market summary slide after slide 2, merge slides 5 and 6, and use a darker theme.

output_examples.input: Create a six-slide overview of AI agents for sales leaders.
output_examples.output: A downloadable PPTX with title, agenda, market context, use cases, risks, and next steps.

output_examples.input: Delete slides 3 and 7 from my local deck and save a new copy.
output_examples.output: A trimmed PPTX saved locally, with a success message listing the removed slides and output path.

output_examples.input: Use my company template to make a customer onboarding deck.
output_examples.output: A new PPTX that follows the uploaded template structure and includes onboarding content.

best_practices: Use absolute paths for local PPTX files and output files.
Confirm whether confidential files may be uploaded before using backend workflows.
Provide slide count, audience, language, style, and required talking points in the first prompt.
anti_patterns: Do not upload sensitive company decks unless external processing is approved.
Do not omit an output path for destructive local edits unless overwriting is intended.
Do not rely on generated decks without reviewing facts, formatting, and speaker context.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Skywork account?
faq.answer: Yes. Backend generation, editing, upload, parsing, and search workflows require Skywork authentication.

faq.question: Can it work fully offline?
faq.answer: Only local slide operations can run without the backend. Generation and AI editing require network access.

faq.question: Can it use my existing PowerPoint template?
faq.answer: Yes. It can upload a PPTX template and generate a new deck using the template style.

faq.question: What file operations are available locally?
faq.answer: It can inspect slide count, delete slides, reorder slides, extract slides, and merge PPTX files.

faq.question: Will my files be uploaded?
faq.answer: Files are uploaded for parsing, template imitation, generation support, and AI editing. Local slide operations do not require upload.

faq.question: What should I check after generation?
faq.answer: Review facts, slide order, formatting, branding, and any charts before sharing the final deck.
